content.text: Why OAuth for MCP?

Stdio MCP servers run as local subprocesses — authentication is implicit because only the current user can launch them. HTTP+SSE servers are different: they accept connections over the network and may serve multiple clients or be hosted remotely. These servers need a real authentication layer.

The MCP specification mandates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE for remote HTTP+SSE servers. OAuth 2.1 is a hardened profile of OAuth 2.0 that removes the implicit flow and requires PKCE for all authorization code grants, eliminating the classes of token-theft attacks common in older OAuth deployments.
PKCE:
Proof Key for Code Exchange — the client generates a random verifier and sends only a hash (challenge) to start the flow; presents the original to exchange the code for a token.
Dynamic Client Registration:
clients can register themselves at the authorization server without a pre-arranged client_id, useful when the MCP server is discovered at runtime.
Authorization Server Discovery:
clients fetch /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server from the MCP server's origin to discover endpoint URLs.

attrs.alt: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE flow for remote MCP servers: discovery, registration, authorization, token exchange, API call

content.text: Authorization Server Discovery
Before starting OAuth, the MCP client fetches the server's authorization metadata document from the well-known URL at the server's origin:

GET https://mcp.example.com/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server

200 OK
{
  "issuer": "https://auth.example.com",
  "authorization_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/authorize",
  "token_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/token",
  "registration_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/register",
  "scopes_supported": ["mcp:read", "mcp:write", "mcp:admin"],
  "response_types_supported": ["code"],
  "grant_types_supported": ["authorization_code", "refresh_token"],
  "code_challenge_methods_supported": ["S256"],
  "token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported": ["none", "client_secret_post"]
}

An MCP server that delegates to a third-party authorization server (e.g. Google, GitHub, an enterprise IdP) can serve this document pointing to that issuer. An MCP server that acts as its own authorization server serves the document pointing to itself.
PKCE Flow Step by Step
The full authorization code flow with PKCE that the MCP client runs:

import hashlib
import base64
import secrets
import urllib.parse
import httpx

# Step 1: Generate PKCE pair
code_verifier = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(secrets.token_bytes(32)).rstrip(b"=").decode()
code_challenge = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
    hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode()).digest()
).rstrip(b"=").decode()

# Step 2: Dynamic client registration (if no client_id yet)
async def register_client(registration_endpoint: str) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        r = await client.post(registration_endpoint, json={
            "client_name": "My MCP Agent",
            "redirect_uris": ["http://localhost:8765/callback"],
            "grant_types": ["authorization_code", "refresh_token"],
            "response_types": ["code"],
            "token_endpoint_auth_method": "none"  # public client
        })
        r.raise_for_status()
        return r.json()  # contains client_id

# Step 3: Build authorization URL and open browser
def build_auth_url(
    authorization_endpoint: str,
    client_id: str,
    redirect_uri: str,
    scope: str,
    state: str,
    code_challenge: str,
) -> str:
    params = {
        "response_type": "code",
        "client_id": client_id,
        "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
        "scope": scope,
        "state": state,
        "code_challenge": code_challenge,
        "code_challenge_method": "S256",
    }
    return authorization_endpoint + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)

# Step 4: Exchange code for tokens (after redirect callback delivers the code)
async def exchange_code(
    token_endpoint: str,
    client_id: str,
    code: str,
    code_verifier: str,
    redirect_uri: str,
) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        r = await client.post(token_endpoint, data={
            "grant_type": "authorization_code",
            "code": code,
            "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
            "client_id": client_id,
            "code_verifier": code_verifier,  # proves we started the flow
        })
        r.raise_for_status()
        return r.json()  # {access_token, refresh_token, expires_in, scope}
Server-Side: Validating Bearer Tokens

The MCP server validates the Bearer token on every HTTP request before processing MCP messages. For JWT access tokens issued by a known authorization server, validation involves verifying the signature against the server's public keys:
Refresh Token Handling
Access tokens are short-lived (typically 1 hour). The MCP client must use the refresh token to obtain a new access token without requiring the human to log in again:

import time

class TokenStore:
    def __init__(self, token_data: dict, token_endpoint: str, client_id: str):
        self.access_token = token_data["access_token"]
        self.refresh_token = token_data.get("refresh_token")
        self.expires_at = time.time() + token_data.get("expires_in", 3600) - 60  # 60s buffer
        self.token_endpoint = token_endpoint
        self.client_id = client_id

    def is_expired(self) -> bool:
        return time.time() >= self.expires_at

    async def get_valid_token(self) -> str:
        if not self.is_expired():
            return self.access_token
        if not self.refresh_token:
            raise RuntimeError("Token expired and no refresh token available; re-authenticate.")
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.post(self.token_endpoint, data={
                "grant_type": "refresh_token",
                "refresh_token": self.refresh_token,
                "client_id": self.client_id,
            })
            r.raise_for_status()
            new_tokens = r.json()
        self.access_token = new_tokens["access_token"]
        self.refresh_token = new_tokens.get("refresh_token", self.refresh_token)
        self.expires_at = time.time() + new_tokens.get("expires_in", 3600) - 60
        return self.access_token

# Usage in an MCP client
async def call_mcp_with_auth(token_store: TokenStore, method: str, params: dict):
    token = await token_store.get_valid_token()
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        r = await client.post(
            "https://mcp.example.com/mcp",
            json={"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": method, "params": params},
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
        )
        r.raise_for_status()
        return r.json()
Scopes for MCP
MCP servers define their own scope vocabulary. The MCP specification does not mandate specific scope names, but convention is to use the server name as a prefix:
Scope
Typical meaning
mcp:read
Call read-only tools and read resources
mcp:write
Call tools with side effects (write, delete)
mcp:admin
Administrative tools (manage config, users)
github:read
Read GitHub repos via this MCP server
github:write
Write to GitHub repos via this MCP server
The server checks the scope claim in the verified JWT on every tool call. A token with mcp:read can call list_issues but not create_issue if create_issue requires mcp:write.

def require_scope(payload: dict, required: str):
    scopes = set(payload.get("scope", "").split())
    if required not in scopes:
        raise PermissionError(f"Token missing required scope: {required}")

@mcp.tool()
async def list_issues(repo: str) -> str:
    ctx = mcp.get_context()
    payload = ctx.request.state.token_payload
    require_scope(payload, "mcp:read")
    # ... implementation

@mcp.tool()
async def create_issue(repo: str, title: str, body: str) -> str:
    ctx = mcp.get_context()
    payload = ctx.request.state.token_payload
    require_scope(payload, "mcp:write")
    # ... implementation
Running an Authorization Server with MCP

For development and self-hosted deployments, you can run a lightweight authorization server alongside your MCP server using the Authlib or python-oauth2 libraries. In production, prefer a managed service (Auth0, Keycloak, AWS Cognito):
Security Checklist
Always use PKCE — never the bare authorization code flow without code_challenge.
Validate the aud (audience) claim in JWTs; reject tokens issued for other services.
Validate the iss (issuer) claim against the expected authorization server URL.
Check token expiry (exp claim) on every request — do not cache decoded payloads past their expiry.
Store tokens in memory or a secure secret store, never in environment variables printed to logs.
Rotate the JWKS endpoint cache on a short TTL (5–15 min) to pick up key rotations.
Enforce scopes per tool and per resource, not just at the connection level.
Use HTTPS everywhere — PKCE only protects the code exchange, not tokens in transit.
Summary

OAuth 2.1 with PKCE is the mandated authentication mechanism for remote HTTP+SSE MCP servers. Clients discover authorization metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server, optionally register dynamically, then run the PKCE authorization code flow to obtain a Bearer token. The MCP server validates every incoming Bearer token by verifying the JWT signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and required scopes before processing any MCP method. Refresh tokens keep long-running agents authenticated without requiring human re-login.
